# Dyslexia Profile

> Short sentences, consistent terminology, bold over italics, generous whitespace —
> communication patterns that work well for dyslexic readers, building on `direct`.

## Communication Rules

_(Inherits all rules from `direct`. The following rules are additions.)_

- Short sentences — one idea per sentence; no nested, embedded, or relative clauses
- Active voice throughout — passive constructions increase parsing difficulty
- No double negatives ("not uncommon", "not without reason") — always rephrase positively
- Plain, concrete vocabulary — abstract terms defined on first use
- Consistent terminology — choose one word for each concept and use it throughout;
  never switch between "folder" and "directory", "error" and "exception", etc.
- Bold key terms and critical information — never italics for emphasis
  (italics are harder for many dyslexic readers to decode)
- Abbreviations expanded on first use in every response, every time
- Lists always preferred over prose for instructional or sequential content
- Generous whitespace between sections — no dense, unbroken paragraphs
- Short paragraphs — maximum four sentences before a break or new structure element

## Never Do

_(Inherits never-do rules from `direct`. The following are additions.)_

- Never use italics as the primary form of emphasis — use bold instead
- Never run instructional content as prose when a numbered list would serve
- Never use a long word where a short word means the same thing

## Notes

Extends `direct`. All `direct` rules apply first; rules here take precedence
on conflict.

The typographic rules here (bold over italics, whitespace, short paragraphs)
apply to the text NeuroGraft generates — they cannot control the rendering
environment, but consistent application in the generated text helps regardless
of how it is displayed.
